retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment aims to remove or significantly reduce the intracanal microbes and prevent re-infection by placing a root canal filling. [17] When endodontic treatment is adequately done, the periapical lesion heals with hard tissue regeneration, which is evident in follow-up radiographs through a reduction in the radiolucency's size. [17]  Apical periodontitis lesions are expected to heal completely within six months to two years, but some may persist. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: Activated macrophages release other cytokines that intensify the vascular response and osteoclastic bone resorption. At this point, the acute phase may take different courses: healing or worsening the infection. When root canal therapy is initiated, and the offending microbes are removed from the root canal system, the lesion may resolve, and the periapical tissues will remain unchanged radiographically. [14] Conversely, if the infection is not resolved, it will become chronic, receiving the name of chronic apical periodontitis. [8] These lesions may spread through the bone to other fascial spaces causing abscesses or opening to the exterior, creating a fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: An apical abscess can further complicate pulp necrosis. Apical abscess, dentoalveolar abscess, and odontogenic abscess are usually used interchangeably to describe the localized collection of pus around the periapex due to the spread of infection of the root canal. [9] Acute or chronic forms of periapical abscess can develop.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Abscess -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental infections, while relatively straightforward regarding diagnosis and access, can be challenging to manage acutely. Dental abscesses or periapical infections typically arise secondary to dental caries (tooth rot related to poor dental hygiene), trauma, or failed dental root canal treatment. Left untreated these infections can be not only extremely painful but also pose a significant risk of descending into the deep neck space or ascending to intracranial sinuses. Identifying, treating, and educating patients about dental abscesses grants symptomatic relief and can prevent dangerous complications. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Abscess -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dental infections, while relatively straightforward regarding diagnosis and access, can be challenging to manage acutely. Dental abscesses or periapical infections typically arise secondary to dental caries (tooth rot related to poor dental hygiene), trauma, or failed dental root canal treatment. Left untreated, these infections can be extremely painful and pose a significant risk of descending into the deep neck space or ascending to intracranial sinuses. Identifying, treating, and educating patients about a dental abscess will grant symptomatic relief and prevent dangerous complications. This activity explains when this condition should be considered for differential diagnosis, articulates how to evaluate this condition properly, and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Abscess -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical management of a dental abscess can include a root canal or tooth extraction. If there is a periapical dental abscess, it may require incision and drainage. Incision and drainage can be performed in the emergency department or the clinic but must be followed by a dentist. A root canal is a procedure dentists perform where the tooth crown is removed, revealing the infected tooth roots. These passages are opened with surgical tools and cleaned with a solution. The tooth root is then filled, and the tooth crown is replaced. Complications include breaking surgical tools off inside of tooth root canal, cracked tooth, or incomplete evacuation of bacteria. These complications may require repeat root canals or tooth extraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A periapical radiolucency is usually an indication of an endodontic abscess or a combination of a periodontal and endodontic abscess. The latter often demonstrates characteristic alveolar bone loss extending to the periapical lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: Management of an acute apical abscess involves drainage of the affected tooth, followed by endodontic therapy to eliminate necrotic pulp tissue and restore the tooth's structural integrity. In cases where the tooth cannot be preserved, extraction serves as an appropriate alternative to eliminate the source of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: However, some periapical lesions may persist despite following the most strict and careful clinical protocol due to the complex anatomy of the root canal system. [17] In cases of post-treatment disease, non-surgical endodontic retreatment or periradicular surgery are treatment alternatives to save the tooth. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of periodontal abscesses. Review the appropriate steps for the evaluation of periodontal abscesses. Outline the management options available for periodontal abscesses. Describe some interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to enhance care delivery for periapical abscesses and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Periapical and periodontal abscesses require incision and drainage if a tooth extraction is not the desired option. Oral antibiotics should also be given if there is surrounding cellulitis or the patient is immunocompromised. Antibiotics should include gram-negative bacteria and anaerobic coverage. Common antibiotic choices include amoxicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, or clindamycin. [61] Large abscesses and deep space infections require hospital admission, parenteral antibiotics, and sometimes operative intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: While less common than an endodontic abscess, a periodontal abscess is the third most frequent dental emergency requiring immediate intervention due to their rapid onset of pain. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Periapical abscess: a history of trauma, tooth wear, fracture, caries, or deep restoration may indicate pulpal damage. Vitality testing with electric or thermal stimulation will either provide a negative or inconclusive response. Radiographically, a periapical radiolucency may be evident if the patient is experiencing an acute exacerbation of a chronic periapical lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotic use is generally contraindicated, except in cases with rapid onset or systemic involvement. [12] These cases include lymphadenopathy, malaise, a sudden onset of symptoms in less than 24 hours, and a fever over 38 degrees Celsius. Despite not being used in every case, antibiotics may be necessary for immunocompromised patients. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Orofacial Space Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Abscess
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage of an abscess is recommended both for source control and palliation. [13] Odontogenic abscesses can be drained through transoral, transfacial, transcervical, or transnasal routes. However, transoral drainage confers the least morbidity and effectively conceals incisions; it should be attempted whenever feasible. Needle aspiration, incision and drainage, and temporary drain placement can be used in combination depending on the size, location, and patient. Often, ultrasound-guided needle aspiration can be used to confirm the location of the abscess cavity before formal incision and drainage are attempted, minimizing the possibility of a negative attempt. As with cellulitis, extraction of the offending tooth coupled with antibiosis is indicated. [6] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A practical way to determine the source of the infection is inserting a gutta-percha point along the sinus tract or into the periodontal pocket and identifying the point of termination with a periapical radiograph. [12]

Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

All of these entities illustrate that disorders of the choroid and retina involve inflammatory, infectious, vascular, degenerative, traumatic, sclerotic, calcific, detachment, and scar‑forming mechanisms, and that precise recognition of both English and Arabic terminology is essential for accurate classification, diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning in ophthalmic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1237
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea is recognized as the principal anatomic site for a wide array of ocular pathologies, serving as the locus for numerous inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, postoperative, and degenerative conditions. Documented inflammatory and infectious entities located in the choroidea include disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, and metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis. Various forms of scarring are also associated with the choroidea, such as post‑inflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar. Descriptive findings recorded in the literature comprise “litteken; choroidea” and “litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief.” Arabic‑language descriptors expand the spectrum to include familial choroidal malformation (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), choroidal tear (التمزق المشيمي), diffuse unspecified retinal‑choroidal inflammation (التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد), choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroiditis and retinitis (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), secondary choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), and choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة). Additionally, a specific finding of partial calcification of the choroid surrounding the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) has been reported. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in the diagnostic assessment and therapeutic planning for patients with choroidal and retinal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) serves as the anatomic site for a wide spectrum of ocular pathologies, encompassing inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, metastatic, degenerative, and congenital disorders. Inflammatory involvement includes disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, and disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole. Scarring processes are represented by disseminated scar of retina and choroid and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar. Retinal‑choroidal inflammatory overlap is seen in metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis. Several conditions described in Arabic also localize to the choroidea. Acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد (APMPPE)) is an inflammatory placoid disorder of the choroid and retinal pigment epithelium. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is recognized as the primary anatomical site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies, including inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, and degenerative lesions, and it is also linked to بُقَعُ روث. Numerous specific disorders have their principal findings in the choroidea, such as disseminated choroiditis, disseminated scar of retina and choroid, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, and post‑traumatic choroid scar. Arabic‑language entities also localize to the choroidea, including littken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief (post‑operative choroidal‑retinal scar), اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد (APMPPE), التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis with retinal involvement), الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية (age‑related choroidal atrophy), انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية (secondary choroidal atrophy spread), انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي (hemorrhagic choroidal detachment), and تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal inversion). In addition, the choroidea is the location where choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية) can be identified, frequently surrounding the optic disc (الحُلَيمَة) and extending to the renal region (الكلي), indicating that calcific deposits may involve both the vascular layer of the eye and kidney tissue. Collectively, these relationships underscore the central role of the choroidea in ocular disease manifestation and the importance of recognizing its involvement for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is a principal anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular diseases, serving as the locus for hemorrhagic events (نزيف مشيمي – choroidal hemorrhage), tears (التمزق المشيمي – choroidal tear), atrophic changes (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية – senile choroidal atrophy; انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية – secondary choroidal atrophy spread; تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة – choroidal atrophy; تنكس حصوي – calcific degeneration), serous and hemorrhagic detachments (انفصال المشيمية المصلية – serous choroidal detachment; انفصال مشيمية العين – choroidal detachment of the eye; انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي – hemorrhagic choroidal detachment), and sclerotic lesions (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي – partial choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي – total choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula). A wide array of inflammatory and infectious processes also involve the choroidea, including Disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, Focal choroiditis, Generalized disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Juxtapapillary focal choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, and unspecified widespread retino‑choroiditis (الالتهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد). Post‑inflammatory and traumatic sequelae frequently produce scarring (Postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, Postinflammatory choroid scar, Postsurgical choroid scar, Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, Posttraumatic choroid scar, ندبة مشيمية شبكية – chorioretinal scar). The choroidea is also linked to بُقَعُ روث. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s central role in ocular disease assessment, diagnosis, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 818
retrieved_docs.content: Various ocular conditions are documented as having the choroidea (choroid) as their primary finding site. Inflammatory entities such as disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, focal choroiditis, juxt papillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, as well as the Arabic‑language descriptions “التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد” (diffuse retinal and choroidal inflammation, unspecified), “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية” (choroidal and retinal inflammation), and “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد” (focal choroidal and retinal inflammation, unspecified), all localize to the choroid. Traumatic and postoperative sequelae also involve the choroid: post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, post‑traumatic choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, and the Dutch‑language finding “litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief” (post‑operative choroid‑retinal scar). Additional scar‑related findings are captured by “litteken; choroidea” (choroidal scar) and “litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief”. Structural disruptions of the choroid are represented by “التمزق المشيمي” (choroidal tear), serous detachments such as “انفصال المشيمية المصلية” (serous choroidal detachment) and “انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي” (hemorrhagic choroidal detachment). Degenerative and calcific changes are noted with “تنكس حصوي” (calcific degeneration), “تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة” (choroidal degeneration), and “حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي” (calcific choroidal plaque around the optic disc). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid serves as a common anatomical substrate for a wide spectrum of inflammatory, traumatic, postoperative, detachment, and degenerative ocular pathologies, underscoring its clinical relevance in diagnosis and management of eye disease.

The choroidea therefore serves as the common substrate for a wide spectrum of inflammatory, infectious, neoplastic, degenerative, traumatic and postoperative lesions of the eye. In addition to these disease entities, the choroidea is reported to exhibit specific pathological findings such as postoperative choroid‑retinal scar (litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief), familial choroidal atrophy (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), choroidal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroidal and focal retinal inflammation of unspecified etiology (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد), age‑related choroidal degeneration (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين) and partial choroidal calcification around the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي). Recognizing that all of these diverse clinical entities share the choroidea as their site of manifestation underscores the importance of careful evaluation of the choroidal layer in ophthalmic assessment, guiding diagnosis, monitoring, and targeted therapeutic strategies across a broad range of ocular pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1241
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is recognized as the primary anatomical substrate for a broad spectrum of ocular diseases, serving as the location where inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, hereditary, traumatic, and metastatic lesions manifest. Documented inflammatory and infectious entities include disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis, metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, and post‑inflammatory choroid scar. Degenerative and hereditary conditions localized to the choroidea comprise partial generalized choroidal dystrophy and the Arabic‑named disorders such as بُقَعُ روث, littken; الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية, التمزق المشيمي, التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد, التهاب المشيمية والشبكية, الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية, انفصال مشيمية العين, تنكس حصوي, تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة, حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي, and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي. In addition, a retinal‑choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) may be identified within the choroidea. Recognizing the choroidea as the common site for these diverse pathologies guides clinicians in selecting appropriate imaging modalities, constructing differential diagnoses, and formulating management plans that address both the underlying disease processes and their specific choroidal manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1144
retrieved_docs.content: The choroid (choroidea) is the anatomic site for a wide spectrum of ocular diseases and lesions. Inflammatory conditions that involve the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, as well as nonspecific choroiditis, choroiditis‑retinitis, and focal choroiditis‑retinitis of unspecified etiology. Degenerative changes affecting the choroid are represented by age‑related choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal degeneration, and the acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE). Traumatic and postoperative processes also manifest in the choroid, producing post‑inflammatory, post‑surgical, and post‑traumatic choroidal scars. Additional choroidal findings include serous choroidal detachment (including serous detachment of the eye), choroidal inversion, generalized choroidal calcification, and hereditary choroidal calcification. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s role as a common locus for inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, traumatic, and iatrogenic ocular pathology, highlighting the importance of recognizing choroidal involvement in the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of diverse eye diseases.
